coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots improving photovoltaic performance of CdS quantum dot-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Ping
Xu, Shunjian
Zhang, Meng
Zhong, Wei
Xiao, Zonghu
Luo, Yongping
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced into CdS quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) to improve the photovoltaic performance. The fluorescent CQDs with average diameter of 2.97 ± 0.4 nm are prepared using cotton fiber as carbon precursor and urea as dopant by hydrothermal method. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of CQDs/CdS based QDSCs is 0.606%, which is 40.9% higher than that of CdS based QDSCs (0.430%). There are three reasons for this improvement: (1) the CQDs broaden the absorption range and enhance the absorption intensity of the sensitized photoanode; (2) the CQDs effectively reduce the charge recombination between the photoanode and electrolyte interface in the QDSCs; (3) the CdS quantum dots (QDs) and CQDs are probably forming a band alignment of type I, which makes the LUMO descend from CdS QDs to TiO2 through CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
CdS quantum Dots
Quantum dot-sensitized solar cells
Hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/CdS and TiO2/CQDs/CdS photoanodes
Morphological and structural characteristics of CQDs and sensitized photoanodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Education Department of Jiangxi Province , China ( KJLD13100 ; GJJ191041 ; GJJ191052 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)TEM image, (b)particle size distribution, (c)XPS survey spectrum, XPS high-resolution scans in (d)C 1s and (e)N 1s, and FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs.
(a-c)SEM morphologies and (d-f)AFM morphologies of TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/CdS and TiO2/CQDs/CdS films, (g-h)TEM morphologies of TiO2/CQDs/CdS, and (i)XRD patterns of different photoanodes.
(a)UV–Vis spectrum of the CQDs, and (b) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the CQDs (Inset: image of CQDs under the UV light of 365 nm).
(a)UV–Vis DRS and (b)PL spectra of different photoanodes, (c)Nyquist plots of different QDSCs, and (d)schematic diagram of CQDs/CdS based QDSCs.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots improving photovoltaic performance of CdS quantum dot-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University, Xinyu, 338004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University, Xinyu 338004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced into CdS quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) to improve the photovoltaic performance. The fluorescent CQDs with average diameter of 2.97 ± 0.4 nm are prepared using cotton fiber as carbon precursor and urea as dopant by hydrothermal method. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of CQDs/CdS based QDSCs is 0.606%, which is 40.9% higher than that of CdS based QDSCs (0.430%). There are three reasons for this improvement: (1) the CQDs broaden the absorption range and enhance the absorption intensity of the sensitized photoanode; (2) the CQDs effectively reduce the charge recombination between the photoanode and electrolyte interface in the QDSCs; (3) the CdS quantum dots (QDs) and CQDs are probably forming a band alignment of type I, which makes the LUMO descend from CdS QDs to TiO2 through CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: As a derivative of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) have been attracting extensive research attention for their high cost-effectiveness and easiness to manufacture [1–3]. Compared with traditional organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) present some charming advantages as light absorbers, including high molar extinction coefficient, tunable band gap, large intrinsic dipole moment and hot electron injection [4–6]. Especially the multiple exciton generation (MEG) effect of QDs pushes the theoretical maximum power conversion efficiency (PCE) of QDSCs up to 44%, which surpasses the Shockley and Queisser limit of 31% for traditional semiconductor solar cells [7,8]. Until now, various kinds of QDs, such as CdS, CdSe, PbS, and CdTe QDs, have been used as sensitizers to construct QDSCs [9–12]. However, all of their power conversion efficiencies are far lower than the maximum theoretical value because of the limited light absorption capacity or incomplete charge separation for single kind of QDs. To solve the above restrictions, on one hand, some methods have been utilized to broaden the light absorption range of QDs. Another kind of QDs was incorporated into the single sensitizer to constitute a co-sensitization system. For example, some co-sensitization systems, such as CdS/CdSe QDs and CdS/PbS QDs, have been widely studied to improve the PCE of QDSCs [2,4,13]. On the other hand, 3D graphene networks prepared by sonication-free liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) and carbon dot grafted graphene hybrids were incorporated into TiO2 photoanodes to solve the problems of insufficient charge separation and collection due to their unique structures and excellent conductivities, and then enhance the photovoltaic performance of QDSCs [14]. It is with regret that the above approaches can't simultaneously address these issues. Hence, it is necessary to propose a method to improve the light absorption capacity and charge separation efficiency of QDSCs at the same time. The incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the QDSCs may achieve this goal.

Because of excellent fluorescence, low toxicity, low cost, wonderful biocompatibility, high chemical stability, and good water solubility, CQDs have got widespread applications in many fields including bioimaging, chemical sensing and photocatalysis et al. [15–17] In addition, owing their fast electron transfer time (<15 fs from CQDs to TiO2), long electron life (about 100 ps), and abundant surface functional groups, CQDs are potential to introduced into QDSCs to effectively enhance the photoelectric performance [18]. Especially, the photoelectric performances of CQDs are controlled by the absorption spectrum, bandgap structure and surface functional group of CQDs [19]. By regulating the micro/macro structure of CQDs, namely the size, internal heteroatom and surface heteroatom functional group, the above three characteristics can be effectively improved [20]. Zhao et al. [21] have broadened light absorption range and accelerated electron transport of CdS quantum dot-sensitized solar cells by introducing Er-doped carbon dots to enhance the photovoltaic performance. In this study, not only is the Er element not needed, but also the deposition of CdS is not as much as Zhang's. However, the PCE of the as-prepared QCSCs is higher. Cotton fiber as carbon precursor and urea as dopant were used to synthesize N-doped CQDs by the hydrothermal method. In the process of co-sensitization, N-doped CQDs/CdS QDs will work together to strengthen the light absorption and facilitate the electron separation at the same time. Therefore, the PCE of QDSCs can be effectively improved through the construction of N-doped CQD/CdS QDs co-sensitized solar cells.

With cotton fiber as carbon precursor and urea as the dopant, fluorescent CQDs are synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal method according to the previous report [19]. 300 mg cotton fiber and 150 mg of urea with 35 mL ultrapure water were transferred into the 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and then heated in the drying oven at 200 °C for 15 h. Once the reaction was completed, the oven was naturally cooled down to room temperature. In order to preliminarily remove the substances that were not dissolved in water, the reactant in the autoclave was sucked out to centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min. Then the supernatant liquor was extracted to filtration with 0.22 μm membranes to further dislodge the residue. The purified CQD solution was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/CdS and TiO2/CQDs/CdS photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the TiO2/CQDs photoanode, the TiO2 photoanode prepared by the previous method [22] was immersed in the CQD solution for 24 h. To get the TiO2/CdS photoanode, the CdS QDs were sensitized to the TiO2 film by the successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method [23]. In this method, the TiO2 photoanode was dipped into 0.02 M Cd(Ac)2 methanol solution for 30 s to absorb Cd2+ ions, and rinsed with methanol to remove the excess Cd2+ ions. Then the photoanode was dried with N2 flow. After that, the photoanode was dipped into 0.02 M Na2S aqueous methanol solution (1:1, v/v) for another 30 s to make the pre-absorbed Cd2+ ions react with S2− ions, resulting in the formation of CdS QDs on the photoanode. Next, the photoanode was rinsed again with methanol before dried with N2 flow. The above two dipping procedures were regarded as one cycle. The amount of CdS QDs deposited on the photoanode can be regulated by changing the number of cycles, and two cycles were used in this research. The TiO2/CQDs photoanode was sensitized with CdS QDs by the SILAR method to acquire the TiO2/CQDs/CdS photoanode.

Na2S·9H2O and S with the mole ratio of 1:1 were dissolved in water through magnetic stirring to constitute 1 M polysulfide electrolyte (S2−| Sn 2−). 7 mM chloroplatinic acidhexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O) in isopropanol(IPA) was pyrolyzed at 400 °C onto the FTO glass to made the counter electrode. The QDSCs were assembled by sandwiching the electrolyte between the sensitized TiO2 photoanode and the counter electrode.

The morphologies of the prepared CQDs and quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 were recorded by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscope (SEM, EVO MA10, Zeiss, Germany) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPM-9600, Shimadzu, Japan) were also used to investigate the morphological properties of the sensitized TiO2 films. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet IS50, Thermo Scientific, USA) spectra were acquired to identify chemical composition and functional groups of the CQDs. X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 advance, Bruker, Germany) were employed to determine the structural characteristics of different photoanodes. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs and sensitized TiO2 films were obtained by a fluorescence spectrometer (Smart Fluo-QY, Zolix, China). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra of the CQDs and UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) of the sensitized TiO2 films are conducted by a spectrophotometer (Cary 100, Agilent, USA). The current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of the QDSCs was performed with Keithley 2400 SourceMeter under AM 1.5 illumination (100 mW cm−2) supplied by a Zolix SS150 solar simulator. The irradiating area of the QDSC was 0.4 cm * 0.4 cm. Incident monochromatic photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) were measured by Solar Cell Scan 100 (Zolix, China) with a standard silicon solar cell as the reference. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was obtained using a electrochemical workstation (CHI650C, Chenhua, China) under dark condition ranging from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of CQDs and sensitized photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM morphology and size distribution of the prepared CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. 1(b), revealing the CQDs are well-separated torispherical nanoparticles with average diameter of 2.97 ± 0.4 nm. The particle sizes of the CQDs are mainly distributed in the narrow range from 2 to 4 nm through the random statistics of 100 particles. XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy are used to investigate the chemical composition and functional groups of the CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs in Fig. 1(c) presents three peaks at 533.0 ev, 401.1 ev and 285.4 ev, which are ascribed to the elements of oxygen (O 1s), nitrogen (N 1s) and carbon (C 1s) respectively. The content of these three elements are shown in the inset of Fig. 1(c). The XPS C 1s spectrum in Fig. 1(d) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 288.1 ev, 286.2 ev, and 284.6 ev, which are attributed to the functional groups of CO, C–N/C–O and CC, respectively [24]. In the high-resolution scan spectra of N 1s from Fig. 1(e), the fitted two peaks at 401.0 ev and 400.0 ev are ascribed to the existence of N–H and pyrrolic N (C–N–C) bands, respectively [25]. The absorption peaks corresponding to the surface functional groups of the CQDs in the FT-IR spectra of Fig. 1(f) agree with the results of the XPS. The broad absorption centered at 3200 cm−1 and 3440 cm−1 can be attributed to the N–H and O–H stretching vibrations of the CQDs [26]. The bands at 1640 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and C–C respectively [22]. The peak at 1600 cm−1 can be ascribed to CC in aromatic ring and CN. The peaks at 1255 cm−1 and 1012 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of C–N, C–OH and C–O–C [27].

In order to determine the existence state of CQDs and CdS QDs in the TiO2 film, TEM, SEM, AFM images and XRD patterns of the quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 films are investigated. It is shown from Fig. 2(a–c) that the TiO2 film are coated with well-distributed quantum dots, which makes electrons transfer easily from conduction band of quantum dots to that of TiO2. Without doubt, the pores in the film are not all blocked because of the sensitized quantum dots, which guarantees the electrolyte penetrate smoothly into the photoanode and reduce the oxidized quantum dots. In addition, the EDX spectra (inset of Fig. 2(a and b)) indicate that the prepared TiO2/CQDs film comprises Ti, C, N, and O elements, while TiO2/CdS film contains Ti, Cd, S and O elements, showing that CQDs and CdS QDs are successfully deposited on the surface of these TiO2 films, respectively. It is found that Ti, Cd, S, C, N, and O elements exist simultaneously in TiO2/CQDs/CdS film from the EDX spectrum in the inset of Fig. 2(c), which further confirms that CdS QDs and CQDs are both deposited on the surface of TiO2 film. Fig. 2(d–f) show AFM morphologies of TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/CdS and TiO2/CQDs/CdS films respectively, which are consistent with the result of Fig. 2(a–c). The surfaces of the sensitized TiO2 films are fairly flat with only small pores. The surface roughness (Ra) of these three films are 217 nm, 261 nm, and 257 nm respectively. Fig. 2(g and h) show TEM images of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film. The lattice fringes of 0.359 nm and 0.238 nm can be observed, which correspond to (101) and (004) planes of anatase TiO2 respectively [28]. Moreover, the lattice fringes of 0.251 and 0.172 are ascribed to (100) planes of CQDs and (311) planes of CdS respectively [29,30]. The diffraction pattern (inset of Fig. 2(g)) shows that the film has polycrystalline rings, which further indicates the presence of these crystalline surfaces. It is evidently concluded that CQDs and CdS QDs are deposited evenly around the TiO2. The crystal structures of the films are also characterized by XRD in Fig. 2(i). With comparison with the XRD patterns of the prepared TiO2, there exist extra diffraction peaks on the TiO2/CQDs/CdS film at 24.5°, 26.8°, 44.1° and 52.4°. The first one can be attributed to (100) lattice plane of CQDs, and the other peaks correspond to (110), (220) and (311) lattice planes of CdS QDs (JCPDS No. 10–0454).

To investigate the effect of the incorporation of CQDs into the TiO2 film on the photoelectric properties of CdS QDSCs, TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/CdS and TiO2/CQDs/CdS films are used as photoanodes to construct QDSCs. J-V curves of different QDSCs are shown in Fig. 3 (a), and Table 1 exhibits the corresponding detailed photovoltaic parameters. For TiO2/CdS QDSC, short-circuit current density (Jsc) and open-circuit voltage (Voc) are 2.52 mA cm−2 and 0.46 V, respectively. Due to the introduction of CQDs to the TiO2 film, Jsc and Voc of TiO2/CQDs/CdS QDSC reach to 2.98 mA cm−2 and 0.51 V, respectively. It can be apparently seen that the CQDs are beneficial to improve the Jsc and Voc of CdS QDSC to some degree. Therefore, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of QDSC grows from 0.430% to 0.606%, with an increase of 40.9%. In addition, it is noted that the photovoltaic performance of TiO2/CQDs QDSC is much lower than that of TiO2/CQDs/CdS QDSC. IPCE spectra are obtained to confirm the light harvesting abilities of these sensitized photoanodes. It is found from Fig. 3(b) that the IPCE values of QDSC based on TiO2/CQDs/CdS are much increased with comparison of QDSC based on TiO2/CdS along the whole spectrum, which ensures the enhancement of photoelectric performance for QDSC based on TiO2/CQDs/CdS. Moreover, the IPCE values between 400 and 500 nm for QDSC based on TiO2/CQDs/CdS are much improved, though IPCE spectrum of the QDSC based on TiO2/CQDs perform badly in this region.

For the sake of finding the improved mechanism of QDSCs, UV–Vis and PL spectra are investigated to identify the optical performances of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the CQD solution has a large absorption of ultraviolet light with a tail extending to the visible region, which is beneficial to the absorption of ultraviolet light for the TiO2/CQDs/CdS film. It is observed that there are two characteristic absorption peaks locating at 238 nm and 357 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum. The former peak is usually ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 segment from the carbon core, while the latter one can be assigned to n-π* transition of CO and other surface moieties [31]. These bands have been proven to exist in the CQDs by XPS and FT-IR. Fig. 4(b) presents the PL emission spectra of the as-prepared CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. With the excitation wavelengths increasing from 270 nm to 410 nm, the emission intensities of the CQDs gradually increase, and then began to decline. When the excitation wavelength is 350 nm, the emission intensity of the CQDs reaches the highest. In addition, the emission peaks exhibit a red shift from 404 nm to 475 nm. The prepared CQDs exhibit fluorescent nature by emitting bright blue light under the UV light of 365 nm (inset of Fig. 4(b)), which confirms the wavelength range of fluorescence emission. The excitation-dependent fluorescence is attributed to the surface functional groups of CQDs, such as CO and C–O, introducing trap states between π-π* states of CC domains [32]. Using quinine sulfate as the standard, the fluorescence quantum yield of the CQDs is measured to be 12.56% at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Combined with the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and image of CQDs under the UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 4(b)), CQDs are indeed a good emitter with bright blue emission. It is noted that the blue light the CQDs emit could be reabsorbed by CdS QDs, and then improve the absorption ability of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film in the visible region.

UV–Vis DRS and PL emission sprectra of the quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 films are used to confirm the effect of CQDs on optical performance of the TiO2/CQDs/CdS film. From the UV–Vis DRS in Fig. 5 (a), the absorption edge of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film is red shifted compared with that of TiO2/CdS film, which broadens the absorption range of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film. This indicates that the band gap of the sensitized TiO2 film is reduced owing to the introduction of CQDs. Moreover, the absorption intensity of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film becomes larger than the TiO2/CdS film after 400 nm. The improvement is ascribed to the fluorescence emitted by CQDs (Fig. 4(b)), which is reabsorbed by CdS QDs. However, the absorption range of TiO2/CQDs film without CdS QDs is very narrow. In order to deeply study the charge transfer process between CdS QDs and CQDs, PL emission spectra of different photoanodes are executed at the same excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 5(b), TiO2/CdS film presents strong emission with two emission peaks at around 470 nm and 558 nm. However, the PL emission intensity of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film is reduced much owing to the incorporation of CQDs into the TiO2/CdS film, suggesting that the recombination rate of electrons and holes is much lower after introducing CQDs to the TiO2 film. The absolute quantum yields of TiO2/CdS, TiO2/CQDs/CdS and TiO2/CQDs films are measured with integrating sphere to be 2.38%, 1.01% and 0.45% respectively. Plus with the emission spectra of these films (Fig. 5(b)), the CQDs act as a bridge to facilitate the rapid transfer of photoexcited electrons from CdS to TiO2, and therefore reduce the CdS emission. EIS is a common technique to investigate the charge transfer process between the interfaces in QDSCs. The Nyquist plots of the different QDSCs are displayed in Fig. 5(c). The big semicircle corresponds to the charge transfer resistance between the photoanode and electrolyte interface. Compared with TiO2/CdS and TiO2/CQDs photoanodes, TiO2/CQDs/CdS photoanode presents the lowest resistance, indicating that the CQDs can effectively inhibit the recombination in the QDSCs. The broadened absorption range and enhanced absorption intensity of the sensitized photoanode, with the effectively reduced charge recombination in the QDSCs, are strongly facilitate the improvement of Jsc for TiO2/CQDs/CdS QDSC. Besides, the energy band structure of the QDSC is investigated to study the synergistic effect between CdS QDs and CQDs. The energy levels of CQDs and CdS are acquired using Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode through CV method in Fig. S1. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) levels of CQDs are −3.95 ev and −5.41 ev respectively, while those of CdS are −3.80 ev and −6.04 ev respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the CdS QDs and CQDs are probably forming a band alignment of type I, which makes the LUMO descend from CdS QDs to TiO2 through CQDs. The CQDs as a bridge between CdS QDs to TiO2 are advantageous to the export of photoelectrons, which reduces the recombination of electrons and holes. In the meanwhile, the CQDs in this band alignment could help improve the Fermi level (EF) of sensitized photoanode, therefore improve Voc of QDSCs.

The prepared CQDs with average diameter of 2.97 ± 0.4 nm emit blue fluorescence, which could be reabsorbed by CdS QDs when the CQDs are introduced into CdS QDSCs. Hence, the absorption ability of the sensitized photoanode is enhanced, which is verified by IPCE spectra. The PL emission intensity of TiO2/CQDs/CdS film is reduced much compared with TiO2/CdS film, combined with the photoluminescence efficiency measurements, suggesting that the recombination rate of electrons and holes is much lower after introducing CQDs to the TiO2 film. The result has also been demonstrated by the EIS test. Based on the energy band structure of the QDSC, the CdS QDs and CQDs are probably forming a band alignment of type I, which makes the LUMO descend from CdS QDs to TiO2 through CQDs. J-V measurements of the QDSCs indicate that the PCE of CQDs/CdS based QDSCs is 0.606%, which is 40.9% higher than that of CdS based QDSCs (0.430%). To sum up, the CQDs could effectively improve the photovoltaic performance of CdS quantum dot-sensitized solar cells.

Ping Huang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Shunjian Xu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Meng Zhang: Supervision. Wei Zhong: Investigation. Zonghu Xiao: Validation. Yongping Luo: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Education Department of Jiangxi Province, China (KJLD13100; GJJ191041; GJJ191052).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reduced charge recombination in a co-sensitized quantum dot solar cell with two different sizes of CdSe quantum dot
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen J, Lei W, Deng W, et al. Reduced charge recombination in a co-sensitized quantum dot solar cell with two different sizes of CdSe quantum dot[J]. Nanoscale, 2011, 3(2): 674-677.

J. Mater. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Peak external photocurrent quantum efficiency exceeding 100% via MEG in a quantum dot solar cell
Science

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dot sensitized solar cells. A tale of two semiconductor nanocrystals: CdSe and CdTe
ACS Nano
